case_metadata.user_request: Find the lowest-energy adsorption configuration for the adsorbate with SMILES [N]=N on the provided surface.

plan.reasoning: No prior attempts exist, so I should propose the best physically motivated initial search point rather than terminate. SMILES consistency check: the adsorbate is [N]=N, i.e. diazenyl-like N2 species with heavy-atom indices 0 and 1. RDKit reports index 0 as N with SP2 hybridization and 1 radical electron, and index 1 as N with SP2 hybridization and 0 radical electrons. There is no user-text/SMILES mismatch to warn about. Because I plan to use a reactive binding mode through atom 0, the radical check is satisfied: atom 0 indeed has radical_electrons > 0, so adsorbate_type should be ReactiveSpecies. For heterogeneous Ta/Sb surfaces, early transition metal Ta is generally much more nitrophilic than metalloid Sb, so the lowest-energy initial guess is most likely adsorption involving Ta rather than Sb. For a diatomic N=N species, a side-on bridge motif across two strong metal atoms is often a good low-energy chemisorption candidate because it can maximize interaction and activate the N=N bond. Among the listed available sites, Ta-Ta bridge is available and is likely stronger than Sb-Ta or ontop sites. The user’s core intent is simply to find the lowest-energy adsorption configuration, so a systematic first attempt should target the most strongly interacting high-symmetry site. Using bridge with two adsorbate binding indices [0,1] is chemically appropriate for side-on N=N adsorption and satisfies the site alignment rule for bridge. I therefore choose a Ta-Ta bridge with both N atoms engaged.

plan.reasoning: SMILES consistency check: the adsorbate is [N]=N, a two-atom nitrogen species. The provided atom list shows atom 0 is N, SP2, degree 1, with 1 radical electron; atom 1 is N, SP2, degree 1, with 0 radical electrons. There is no mismatch between the SMILES and the intended adsorbate description. Because one binding atom has an unpaired electron, treating this as a ReactiveSpecies is chemically justified if we bind through atom 0. History analysis: only one successful plan exists so far, initially set up as bridge on Ta-Ta using adsorbate indices [0,1], and it relaxed to a hollow Ta-rich site with very strong adsorption energy of -9.669 eV, which is currently the best. No [🔄 Converged to known best] tag is present, so termination is not yet forced. To remain systematic, the next plan must be different from the previous one. Since the known best final state is very Ta-rich, Ta-centered motifs are likely favorable. The major untested high-symmetry alternatives among the allowed site families are ontop Ta, ontop Sb, bridge Sb-Ta, and hollow Sb-Ta-Ta / Ta-Ta-Ta as distinct initializations. For N2-like species, an end-on mode through the radical-bearing N atom is a physically reasonable alternative to the previously tested side-on/di-sigma setup. This tests whether a distinct chemisorption minimum exists without repeating the prior Ta-Ta bridge dual-binding plan. User intent is to find the lowest-energy adsorption configuration, so the next most informative new trial is an end-on Ta ontop adsorption through atom 0. This uses the correct adsorbate index from the provided list and respects all site/alignment rules.

plan.reasoning: SMILES consistency check: the adsorbate is [N]=N with two heavy atoms. RDKit indices are N(0) and N(1), both SP2; atom 0 has one radical electron and atom 1 has zero. There is no mismatch between user request and SMILES. Because the species is written as a radical-like diazenyl form rather than closed-shell N2, if I use a reactive binding mode I must bind through atom 0, the only atom with an unpaired electron. History analysis: two successful plans already exist. A Ta-Ta bridge initialization using adsorbate indices [0,1] relaxed to a hollow Ta-rich site and gave the current best energy of -9.669 eV. A Ta ontop initialization also relaxed to a hollow Ta-rich site with -9.354 eV. No failures are listed, and there is no [🔄 Converged to known best] tag. Since major Ta-centered motifs have been sampled and both drifted toward hollow Ta environments, the remaining chemically distinct unexplored high-symmetry option most likely to compete is an Sb-containing hollow/near-hollow environment, because this is a heterogeneous Ta/Sb surface and alloy site fingerprints can differ significantly. To avoid repeating prior plans, I should not use Ta-Ta bridge or Ta ontop again. The most systematic new test is a hollow site composed of Sb-Ta-Ta. User intent is simply to find the lowest-energy adsorption configuration, so exploring the main untested mixed hollow motif is appropriate. For adsorption type, [N]=N can reasonably adsorb as a complete molecular species in side-on fashion using both nitrogens at a hollow site; this does not require both atoms to be radicals, unlike ReactiveSpecies. Therefore I choose Molecule with binding indices [0,1].

plan.reasoning: SMILES consistency check: the adsorbate is [N]=N with two heavy atoms. The provided atom list is consistent with diazenyl-like NN: atom 0 is N, SP2, degree 1, radical_electrons 1; atom 1 is N, SP2, degree 1, radical_electrons 0. Because one selected binding atom can be atom 0 with an unpaired electron, a ReactiveSpecies treatment is chemically justified. History analysis: three plans have already been tested. (1) bridge on Ta-Ta using indices [0,1] succeeded and relaxed to a hollow HCP/subsurface-like configuration with the current best energy -9.669 eV. (2) ontop on Ta using index [0] succeeded and relaxed to an FCC-like hollow with energy -9.354 eV. (3) hollow on Sb-Ta-Ta using indices [0,1] dissociated, so that is a failed plan despite the low energy. Therefore, a new plan must differ from all prior initial plans. Surface options still untested include ontop on Sb, bridge on Sb-Ta, and hollow on Ta-Ta-Ta. Since the alloy is heterogeneous and Sb-containing environments have not been cleanly sampled except for one dissociative hollow case, a chemically distinct mixed bridge Sb-Ta is the most systematic next test. For N2-like species on transition-metal-rich surfaces, side-on two-point coordination is often favorable, and testing a mixed bridge can reveal whether Sb perturbs Ta binding beneficially or destabilizes adsorption. I avoid repeating Ta-Ta bridge, Ta ontop, and Sb-Ta-Ta hollow because those were already attempted or led to failure. User intent is to find the lowest-energy adsorption configuration, so the next best unexplored high-symmetry motif is a side-on bridge over Sb-Ta. Adsorbate binding indices must strictly use the provided list, so I choose [0,1].

plan.reasoning: SMILES consistency check: the adsorbate is [N]=N with heavy atom indices 0 and 1, both N atoms are SP2. One N atom (index 0) has 1 radical electron, while index 1 has 0 radical electrons. There is no mismatch between the requested adsorbate and the SMILES; I will proceed based strictly on this SMILES and the provided indices. Because I plan to adsorb the intact N2-like species rather than a detached radical fragment, I treat it as a Molecule; using both N atoms in a side-on molecular adsorption mode is chemically reasonable for an unsaturated N=N species on a metal surface. History analysis: tested successful plans are bridge on Ta-Ta with adsorbate indices [0,1], which relaxed to a very stable hollow-like Ta-rich site at -9.669 eV and is currently the best, and ontop on Ta with one N bound at -9.354 eV. Failed plans are hollow on Sb-Ta-Ta and bridge on Sb-Ta, both of which dissociated and are therefore not acceptable despite one having a low energy. No convergence tag [🔄 Converged to known best] is present, so termination is not yet mandated. To remain systematic and propose a completely new plan, I should avoid repeating bridge Ta-Ta, ontop Ta, hollow Sb-Ta-Ta, and bridge Sb-Ta. The remaining major untested high-symmetry options from the available site list are ontop Sb and hollow Ta-Ta-Ta. Since Ta-rich environments have already given the strongest intact adsorption, the most promising unexplored option is a Ta-Ta-Ta hollow with side-on N=N binding through both N atoms. This tests whether a true threefold Ta hollow can outperform the previous Ta-Ta bridge-start that relaxed into a hollow-like geometry. User intent is to find the lowest-energy intact adsorption configuration for [N]=N on the Ta/Sb surface. Therefore I will continue with a new hollow Ta-Ta-Ta plan using adsorbate binding indices [0,1].
